summary:
Catfish Cave (Djara Abu Tanqura Bahari) is a prehistoric cave site in the Western Desert of Egypt, in the Farafra Oasis area, with evidence of occupation during the early Holocene when the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] created more favorable conditions in what is now among the driest places on earth. The site has yielded paintings and artifacts associated with communities inhabiting the Egyptian Western Desert during the early phase of the Green Sahara.

The rock paintings at Catfish Cave depict various animals including the catfish (Synodontis schall) from which the site takes its name, as well as other fauna associated with the wetter conditions of the early Holocene. The site contributes to the picture of a more densely inhabited, ecologically diverse Egyptian interior before the progressive desiccation of the Sahara forced populations toward the Nile Valley. Sites such as Catfish Cave and the [[event:1292|Nabta Playa]] assemblage document the broader pattern of Saharan habitation during the African Humid Period and the cultural lives of the communities who occupied this now-barren region when it was habitable.
